rainfall_taxonomy_summary.key_insight: Of 20 El Nino years (1951-2025), 8 had deficient monsoons (40%), 11 had normal monsoons (55%), and 1 had excess (5%). El Nino raises drought probability from base rate ~16% to ~40%, but majority of El Nino years still saw normal rainfall.

caveats: Based on 28 analog years — small sample, interpret with caution
Past distributions are not guarantees of future outcomes
El Nino years with clean data: n=8 for LOW bucket, n=11 for NORMAL

gdp_drag_estimate.note: GDP drag = agri_share × (actual_agri_growth - trend). Negative = headwind to GDP.

cross_check.description: 1% shortfall in monsoon rainfall → GDP impact
cross_check.note: Non-linear: severe drought (-20%+ deficit) has larger multiplier due to food inflation → RBI rate hikes → broader demand compression

published_sensitivities.source: RBI Annual Report 2019-20, Economic Survey 2023-24, various RBI staff papers

monsoon_gdp_sensitivity.description: 1% shortfall in monsoon rainfall → GDP impact
monsoon_gdp_sensitivity.note: Non-linear: severe drought (-20%+ deficit) has larger multiplier due to food inflation → RBI rate hikes → broader demand compression

drought_food_inflation.description: Drought year impact on food inflation
drought_food_inflation.examples: 2009: +14.4%, 2015: modest due to global commodity weakness

drought_rbi_response.description: RBI response to drought-driven food inflation
drought_rbi_response.typical: Holds rates or delays cuts; rarely hikes solely for food inflation
drought_rbi_response.note: 2014-2016 cycle shows tolerance of food inflation if core is contained

disclaimers: This is personal research, NOT investment advice.
All figures are conditional distributions from historical analogs — past performance does not predict future results.
Sample sizes are small (n=8 for El Nino drought years). Interpret with extreme caution.
Individual stock analysis uses sector proxy data, not actual stock-level returns.
The 2026 probability weights are model estimates that will shift as the season progresses.
